learning_objectives: Formulate research questions appropriate for international studies
Distinguish between qualitative, quantitative, and mixed methods approaches
Understand the relationship between theory, hypotheses, and evidence
readings: King, Keohane & Verba, Designing Social Inquiry Ch. 1-2
George & Bennett, Case Studies and Theory Development Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Research methods provide the tools for systematically investigating questions about international politics, economics, and society. Mastering them transforms you from a consumer of research to a producer.
key_concepts: Research question formulation: from puzzle to question to hypothesis
Ontology and epistemology in social research
Qualitative vs. quantitative: different questions, not better/worse
Research design: case study, comparative, statistical, experimental
The centrality of causal inference: how do we know X causes Y?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Transforming a policy puzzle into a researchable question with testable hypotheses
setup_requirements: Example research questions from published studies

review.value: The strategic plan that connects research questions to data collection and analysis, ensuring that evidence can actually answer the question asked
The challenge of determining that X causes Y rather than merely correlating with it, requiring attention to confounders, selection effects, and reverse causation

class_schedule.topic: Case Study Methods and Process Tracing
learning_objectives: Design single and comparative case studies
Apply process tracing to identify causal mechanisms
Evaluate the strengths and limitations of case study research

lecture_notes.introduction: Case studies are the workhorse of qualitative international studies research. When done well, they can identify causal mechanisms that statistical methods miss.
key_concepts: Case selection: typical, deviant, most-likely, least-likely cases
Within-case analysis: process tracing causal chains
Types of process tracing: theory-testing, theory-building, explaining-outcome
Causal mechanism identification: the smoking gun test
Case study limitations: generalizability, selection bias
lecture_notes.demonstration: Process tracing exercise: tracing the causal chain from economic sanctions to regime behavior change

review.value: A qualitative method that traces the causal chain between an independent variable and the outcome within a single case, identifying the intervening steps (mechanisms) through which causation operates
The process or pathway through which a cause produces an effect -- process tracing looks inside the 'black box' of causation to identify these step-by-step linkages

class_schedule.topic: Quantitative Methods: Statistics for International Studies
learning_objectives: Understand the logic of statistical inference for large-N analysis
Interpret regression analysis and its application to international studies
Evaluate the strengths and limitations of quantitative approaches

lecture_notes.introduction: Quantitative methods allow us to identify patterns across many cases and test whether relationships are statistically robust. Understanding them is essential even for qualitative researchers.
key_concepts: Descriptive statistics: measures of central tendency and dispersion
Correlation vs. causation: the fundamental challenge
Regression analysis: OLS, logistic regression, and interpretation
Statistical significance, confidence intervals, and p-values
Common pitfalls: omitted variable bias, endogeneity, overfitting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hands-on regression exercise: analyzing the relationship between democracy level and conflict onset using cross-national data

review.value: A statistical method estimating the relationship between dependent and independent variables while controlling for other factors, the workhorse of quantitative IR research
The distortion that occurs when a relevant variable is left out of a statistical model, causing the estimated effect of included variables to be biased

class_schedule.topic: Qualitative Data Collection: Interviews, Archives, and Ethnography
learning_objectives: Design and conduct elite interviews for international studies research
Evaluate archival sources and their limitations
Assess ethnographic methods for studying international phenomena

lecture_notes.introduction: Qualitative data does not collect itself. Effective interviewing, archival research, and ethnographic observation require training, preparation, and ethical awareness.
key_concepts: Elite interviewing: design, execution, and analysis
Semi-structured vs. structured interviews
Archival research: diplomatic records, declassified documents, organizational files
Ethnographic methods: participant observation in international settings
Research ethics: informed consent, do no harm, positionality
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interview design workshop: developing an interview protocol for a research project on diplomatic negotiations

review.value: A semi-structured interview with a knowledgeable informant (diplomat, official, expert) that combines predetermined questions with flexibility to follow up on unexpected insights
Special ethical considerations when conducting research in conflict zones or with vulnerable populations: informed consent, do no harm, confidentiality, positionality, and power dynamics

learning_objectives: Design mixed-methods research that integrates qualitative and quantitative approaches
Evaluate the strengths of multimethod triangulation
Assess when and why to combine methods
readings: Lieberman, 'Nested Analysis as a Mixed-Method Strategy for Comparative Research'

lecture_notes.introduction: The most compelling international studies research often combines methods. Mixed methods leverage the strengths of each approach while compensating for their individual weaknesses.
key_concepts: Sequential mixed methods: qual -> quant or quant -> qual
Concurrent mixed methods: parallel data collection and analysis
Nested analysis: combining large-N statistical analysis with in-depth case studies
Triangulation: using multiple methods to converge on findings
When to use mixed methods: complementarity, expansion, confirmation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Research design exercise: converting a single-method study into a mixed-methods design

review.value: Lieberman's strategy of embedding case studies within a large-N statistical framework: statistics identify patterns, case studies explain mechanisms and investigate outliers
Using multiple methods, data sources, or theoretical perspectives to examine the same phenomenon, increasing confidence when findings converge across approaches

class_schedule.topic: Writing, Presenting, and Publishing International Studies Research
learning_objectives: Structure a research paper for international studies audiences
Understand the academic publication process and policy writing
readings: Van Evera, Guide to Methods for Students of Political Science Ch. 6
Belcher, Writing Your Journal Article in Twelve Weeks Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Research is only complete when it is communicated. Writing well is as important as researching well, and the conventions of academic and policy writing differ significantly.
key_concepts: Research paper structure: introduction, literature review, theory, methods, findings, conclusion
The literature review as argumentative positioning, not bibliography
Academic vs. policy writing: different audiences, different conventions
Presenting research: conference talks, seminar presentations, policy briefings
The publication process: peer review, revision, and response
lecture_notes.demonstration: Peer review exercise: reviewing and providing constructive feedback on classmates' draft research proposals
setup_requirements: Research proposal draft from each student

review.value: Not a summary of everything written on a topic but an argumentative positioning of your research within existing scholarship: identifying gaps, debates, and the contribution your work makes

Policy briefs lead with recommendations, use plain language, and are 2-4 pages. Academic papers lead with a puzzle, engage theory, detail methodology, and can be 30+ pages. Both require rigor but serve different audiences.

questions.question: Explain the difference between a most-likely and a least-likely case study design. When would you use each, and what kind of inference does each support?
questions.answer: A most-likely case is one where the theory being tested has the best chance of being confirmed: if the theory fails here, it is seriously weakened. A least-likely case is one where the theory is unlikely to hold: if the theory succeeds here, it is strongly supported. Use a most-likely design to challenge a theory (if it fails where it should succeed, doubt it). Use a least-likely design to confirm a theory (if it works where it shouldn't, trust it more). Most-likely cases support disconfirmation (theory fails its best test). Least-likely cases support confirmation (theory passes its hardest test). Example: testing whether democracies cooperate more -- the US-UK relationship (most-likely) or US-Russia cooperation on nuclear arms control (least-likely for the democratic cooperation thesis).

rubric.key: Identifies the type of inference each supports

questions.question: What is process tracing and how does the 'smoking gun' test work? Provide a brief example from international studies.
questions.answer: Process tracing is a qualitative method that examines the causal chain within a single case to identify the mechanism linking cause to effect. It opens the 'black box' between X and Y by identifying the intermediate steps. The smoking gun test looks for a piece of evidence that is highly diagnostic: if found, it strongly confirms the hypothesis (high uniqueness: only one theory predicts this evidence), though its absence doesn't necessarily disconfirm it (low certainty). Example: in studying whether economic sanctions caused Iran to negotiate on its nuclear program, a smoking gun would be internal Iranian government documents showing leaders explicitly decided to negotiate because sanctions were economically devastating -- highly diagnostic because it directly reveals the causal mechanism, though absence of such documents doesn't prove sanctions were irrelevant.

rubric.key: Provides an apt international studies example

questions.question: What is omitted variable bias and why is it a fundamental challenge for quantitative international studies research? Provide an example.
questions.answer: Omitted variable bias occurs when a variable that affects both the independent and dependent variables is left out of a statistical model, causing the estimated relationship between the included variables to be biased. It is fundamental because in observational social science research (as opposed to experiments), we can never be certain we have included all relevant variables. Example: a study finds that countries with more McDonald's restaurants have fewer civil wars, suggesting economic development prevents conflict. But both variables are driven by an omitted variable: state capacity. Countries with effective governments can maintain both franchise businesses and domestic peace. Without controlling for state capacity, the correlation between McDonald's and peace is spurious.

rubric.key: Explains why it's a fundamental challenge

questions.question: Explain the logic of nested analysis (Lieberman). How does combining large-N analysis with case studies produce stronger research than either method alone?
questions.answer: Nested analysis combines large-N statistical analysis with in-depth case studies in a sequential design. The researcher first runs statistical models on a large dataset to identify patterns and relationships. Then selects cases for deeper qualitative analysis based on the statistical results: on-the-line cases (to explore causal mechanisms behind the statistical relationship), outlier cases (to investigate why some cases deviate from the pattern), and influential cases (to check whether the results are robust). This is stronger because: statistics identify generalizable patterns but cannot reveal causal mechanisms; case studies identify mechanisms but cannot establish generalizability. Together, statistics show that X and Y are correlated across many cases, and case studies show how and why X causes Y in specific instances. Outlier analysis can also reveal omitted variables or scope conditions that refine the theory.

rubric.key: Explains the logic of nested analysis
Describes how cases are selected from statistical results

major_assignments.title: Original Research Proposal
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete research proposal for an original study in international studies, including research question, literature review, theoretical framework, methodology, and preliminary evidence or pilot analysis.
objectives: Formulate an original, researchable question in international studies
Situate the question within existing scholarship

criteria: Connected to policy or theoretical importance
Literature review positions the study within existing research
Hypotheses or expectations derived from theory
Research design appropriate for the question
Case selection or sampling strategy justified
Data collection plan is specific and feasible

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on research methods, design, and analysis

methods_exercises.description: Hands-on exercises in qualitative and quantitative methods

midterm_examination.description: Exam on qualitative and quantitative methods

final_examination.description: Methods application exam with real data

course_policies.attendance: Attendance mandatory for all workshop sessions. Missing a methods workshop results in zero for that exercise.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late proposals penalized 10% per day. No extensions within 72 hours of deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Methods exercises may be collaborative. The research proposal must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Fabrication of data or results constitutes academic misconduct and results in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Statistical software (R or Stata), qualitative analysis software (NVivo or Atlas.ti), and reference management tool.
